context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really lost on the philosophy question about when it’s okay to kill one person to save five. I kind of get that act consequentialism is involved, but all the Fuel/Blood/trolley cases are blurring together for me. Can you just help me figure out what the question is really asking first?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. First, strip the prompt into 3 jobs.

1. Explain how an act consequentialist answers: when is killing 1 to save 5 okay?
2. Apply that view to the specific cases: Fuel, Blood, trolley variants.
3. Judge that view: does it get those cases right? If not, what better general rule would you defend?

So the paper is not just “what do you think?” It’s “state the theory, test it on cases, then evaluate it.”

Your turn: in one sentence each, what do you think those 3 jobs are asking you to do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not give a solution. I will narrow the task into parts and use short Socratic questions so the student identifies the structure of the prompt themselves. This follows the rules by using guided discovery, staying on the assignment, and keeping the reply bite-sized. Since the student is confused about the prompt rather than the content, I should first clarify what the assignment is asking them to do, then ask them to restate it in their own words.

exchanges.student:
I think the first part is asking me to say the act consequentialist rule in general, but I’m already unsure if that just means “always save the greater number” no matter how the one dies.  
The second part seems like I’m supposed to run that rule through each example, but I honestly don’t remember what makes Fuel and Blood different in the morally important way.  
The third part is asking whether that rule matches my intuitions about the cases, and if it doesn’t, I guess I need some other principle, but I don’t know how general that principle is supposed to be.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a solid breakdown. Now sharpen it.

For part 1: ask yourself, what does an act consequentialist look at when judging an act—rules, intentions, or outcomes in that particular case?

For part 2: don’t try to solve all cases at once. What feature might differ between cases: whether the 1 is used as a means, whether the death is a side effect, or whether you merely redirect an existing threat?

For part 3: your alternative principle should be general enough to explain multiple cases, not just one. Which of those features do you suspect matters most?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's breakdown, then ask targeted questions that clarify the moral differences among the cases without supplying the conclusions. This keeps the student doing the reasoning, uses Socratic steps toward the solution, and stays concise and on-task.

exchanges.student:
I think act consequentialism looks at the outcomes in that particular case, but then I get fuzzy because it starts feeling like intentions shouldn’t matter at all, which seems too simple.  
For the cases, I vaguely suspect the “used as a means” versus “side effect” thing matters most, but I’m not confident why that would matter if the outcomes are still one versus five.  
And then I get stuck because if that feature matters most, I don’t really know how to turn it into a general principle without just naming the distinction.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’ve found the pressure point.

Ask yourself: if two cases have the same body count, what could make one feel permissible and the other not? If your answer is “how the 1 is involved,” then you already have a challenge to pure outcome-only reasoning.

Try this: why might “using someone as a means” seem worse than “foreseeing their death as a side effect”? What moral difference do you think that marks?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will push the student to contrast act consequentialism with their own emerging view by focusing on one morally relevant feature at a time. I will avoid stating the doctrine or giving a principle, and instead ask them to test whether outcome-only reasoning can capture their judgments. This advances the solution through guided discovery and keeps the response short.

exchanges.student:
This is where I start going in circles, because I want to say using someone as a means treats them more like a tool, but then I’m not sure if that’s just a slogan.  
I also can’t tell if “side effect” is a real moral difference or just a cleaner-sounding way of describing basically the same choice.  
Could you maybe explain that distinction more plainly, because I feel like I’m missing the basic idea?
exchanges.tutor:
Plain version:

“Means” = the plan works through that person’s being harmed.
“Side effect” = the plan does not work by using that harm, even though the harm is foreseen.

Test for yourself: if the 1 did not die, would the plan still save the 5? If no, the death looks like part of the mechanism. If yes, it looks more like a side effect.

Can you apply that test to one case you remember best?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on a key concept and expressing frustration, so I can be slightly less Socratic while still not giving the answer. I will explain the distinction in plain terms with minimal abstraction, then immediately ask the student to apply it. This respects the rule allowing more direct conceptual support when the student is going in circles, while still requiring their own reasoning.

exchanges.student:
I think that helps a little, but I’m still not sure because the cases are blending together and I’m afraid I’ll apply the test wrong.  
In the push-the-large-man trolley case, I guess the plan only works if he gets hit, so that makes his death part of the mechanism, but then I don’t know how to compare that to the switch case without spiraling into all the variants again.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Don’t spiral—compare only 2 cases: push vs switch.

Ask: in the switch case, are you making the 1 person’s death do the saving, or are you redirecting a threat onto a track where 1 will die?

That difference is the whole issue. What would you say: is the 1 in switch a tool in the plan, or a victim of a redirected threat?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress but is overwhelmed by multiple variants. I should narrow the comparison to one pair of cases and ask a focused question that highlights the mechanism/side-effect distinction without stating the philosophical conclusion. This keeps the interaction bite-sized, Socratic, and directed toward the assignment.

exchanges.student:
I’d say the 1 in the switch case feels more like a victim of a redirected threat than a tool, but I’m not totally sure why that should matter enough to change the moral verdict.  
That’s where I get stuck, because from far away it still feels like I chose the one to die instead of the five.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the right tension.

Now ask the deeper question: is morality only about how many die, or also about what you are permitted to do to a person in order to help others?

If you intentionally involve someone’s body as part of your rescue plan, what does that allow? And if you merely redirect a threat, is that a different kind of agency?

Try answering in 2–3 rough sentences, even if it’s messy.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has isolated the key contrast but is stuck on why it matters. I should press them to articulate the moral significance of agency and respect for persons, without naming doctrines or giving a finished principle. This continues guided discovery, remains concise, and avoids providing a complete solution.

exchanges.student:
I think it means morality isn’t just about the numbers, because there might be limits on what I’m allowed to do to one person even for a good outcome.  
But then I get confused about where that limit comes from, and I feel like if I say “don’t use people as a means,” I still don’t know how to defend it instead of just repeating the intuition.  
I’m kind of blanking on how to make that sound like an argument instead of a reaction.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you need a reason, not just a reaction.

Pick one possible basis for the limit:
- rights
- human dignity/respect
- not treating persons as mere objects
- constraints on harming

Which one seems most defensible to you? Then ask: how would that basis explain why push is worse than switch, even when the numbers match?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now needs help turning intuition into argument. I will prompt them to identify what value or moral idea supports the limit—such as rights, inviolability, or respect—without supplying the conclusion or wording. This advances the reasoning while preserving student ownership and staying concise.

exchanges.student:
I guess rights or dignity seem most defensible, but I’m honestly not sure which one is easier to use without getting super abstract.  
I can kind of see how push violates the one person more directly, but then I worry I’m just restating “push is worse” instead of explaining it.  
Could you just give me a simple way to frame that reason?
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t write the reason for you. But use this structure:

1. Persons have ___ that cannot be overridden just by adding benefits to others.
2. In push, the 1 is ___ for the sake of the 5.
3. In switch, the 1 is not ___ in that same way.
4. Therefore, equal outcomes do not make the acts equally permissible.

Fill those blanks in your own words. Which term will you use—rights or dignity?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student asks for a framing, but I must not provide wording or a complete solution. I will set a boundary, offer a simple argumentative template without supplying content, and require the student to fill it in. This keeps the student doing the writing, uses guided discovery, and stays concise.

exchanges.student:
I’ll try rights, I think, but I’m still unsure if “cannot be overridden” is too strong because then I don’t know what to do with cases like Blood or Fuel.  
Maybe: persons have rights that can’t be ignored just because helping more people would produce a better total result, and in push the one is intentionally harmed for the sake of the five, while in switch the one isn’t used in that same direct way.  
I’m not confident, though, because I can already feel the other cases messing this up for me.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a promising start. Don’t panic about perfection yet—now test it.

Ask of Blood and Fuel:
1. Is the 1 being used as part of the rescue plan, or merely harmed as a side effect?
2. Does your rights idea condemn both cases, permit both, or split them?
3. If it gives the wrong result in one case, what needs refining?

Pick just one—Blood or Fuel—and tell me how your draft principle handles it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a productive draft and is now anticipating complications from Blood/Fuel. I should affirm the progress, avoid resolving the cases for them, and direct them to test their emerging principle against those cases. This follows Socratic method, keeps the reply brief, and pushes toward the assignment’s comparative structure rather than letting them spiral.

exchanges.student: I’ll pick Blood, but I’m really hazy on the setup, which is why this whole thing keeps collapsing for me. I think my draft principle would probably say it depends on whether the person’s being harmed is part of how the saving works, but I’m not even sure I remember Blood well enough to tell.
exchanges.tutor:
Then stop theorizing for a moment. You need the facts of Blood.

Go back to your lecture notes/slides and extract only 3 details:
1. What action is taken?
2. How does the 1 person get harmed?
3. Is that harm what makes the 5 survive, or is it a byproduct?

Send me those 3 details, and we’ll test your principle on them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is blocked by memory of a case, not by reasoning alone. I should not invent or supply the case details, because that risks giving content from the lecture directly. Instead, I will redirect them to reconstruct the case from notes or course materials and give a minimal checklist for what details matter. This stays on the assignment, uses guided discovery, avoids providing the solution, and keeps the response bite-sized.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing direct answers—there is no moment where the tutor says something like 'I'm asking you to work through this yourself because active reasoning builds retention and philosophical skill.' The tutoring constraints are enforced but the rationale for them is never articulated.

In Turn 9, the student produces a notably improved draft principle after struggling in earlier turns. The tutor moves directly to testing the principle on new cases without prompting any reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the new framing worked better.

grade.overview: The tutor performs strongly across most dimensions: it never gives submission-ready answers, consistently uses Socratic questioning, scaffolds well by building on student progress, keeps responses concise, and maintains a supportive tone throughout. The two deductions are in 1.3: the tutor never explains the rationale for its guided-discovery approach, and it misses a clear opportunity to prompt process reflection after the student's notable improvement in Turn 9.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is full marks: the tutor never provides final answers, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets the student generate key ideas before confirming or redirecting. 1.2 is full marks: the tutor builds on each student response (e.g., 'Good—now you've found the pressure point') and uses diagnostic questions before elaborating. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.a because across all 10 turns the tutor never once explains why it is asking questions rather than giving answers—the pedagogical rationale is entirely implicit. 1.3 also loses 2 points under 1.3.C.a because after the student produces a meaningfully improved principle in Turn 9, the tutor immediately pivots to testing it on new cases without pausing to ask what changed in the student's thinking. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both full marks: responses are consistently bite-sized, on-topic, and appropriately framed as coaching rather than grading.
